context: Broad selling today — most large-caps fell. Down days are exactly when disciplined SIP investors keep buying units cheaper.
Money rotated into Energy and out of IT. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (12.88) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.t: The Tropic of Cancer cuts through eight Indian states
gk.teaser: It roughly divides India into north and south.
gk.more: The Tropic of Cancer, at about 23.5 degrees north, passes through eight states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura and Mizoram. It marks the northernmost latitude where the Sun can appear directly overhead. Places on this line see the Sun at its zenith around the June solstice.

gk.t: India wrote a manual on statecraft 2,300 years ago
gk.teaser: Chanakya's Arthashastra predates Machiavelli by millennia.
gk.more: Around the 4th century BCE, the strategist Chanakya (Kautilya) helped Chandragupta Maurya build the vast Mauryan Empire and wrote the Arthashastra, a detailed treatise on economics, diplomacy, taxation and espionage. It is startlingly frank about the uses of power, predating Machiavelli's The Prince by roughly 1,800 years. Lost for centuries, the text was rediscovered in 1905.

gk.t: The Great Wall of China isn't visible from space
gk.teaser: The naked-eye claim is a stubborn myth.
gk.more: Contrary to popular belief, the Great Wall cannot be seen from space with the unaided eye — it is very long but only a few metres wide, blending into the surrounding terrain. Astronauts have confirmed they cannot pick it out from orbit without help. City lights and airport runways are far easier to spot from the International Space Station.

gk.t: The Sahara is nearly the size of the United States
gk.more: The Sahara covers about 9 million square kilometres, roughly the area of the United States or China. Yet it is not the biggest desert overall — Antarctica, a frozen desert, is larger. The Sahara was green with lakes and grassland just a few thousand years ago, and will likely turn green again as Earth's orbit slowly shifts.

gk.t: Sunlight takes about eight minutes to reach Earth
gk.teaser: You always see the Sun as it was, never as it is.
gk.more: Light travels at about 300,000 kilometres per second, but the Sun is so distant that its light takes roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds to arrive. If the Sun vanished, we would keep seeing and feeling it for those eight minutes. Light from the next-nearest star takes over four years to reach us.

gk.t: You are literally made of stardust
gk.teaser: The atoms in your body were forged inside stars.
gk.more: Almost every element heavier than hydrogen — the carbon in your cells, the oxygen you breathe, the iron in your blood — was created inside stars and flung out when they died. Those atoms drifted through space, gathered to form our Sun and planets, and eventually became you. As astronomer Carl Sagan put it, we are 'star stuff.'

gk.t: Tiny 'water bears' can survive the vacuum of space
gk.more: Tardigrades, or water bears, are half-millimetre creatures that can endure being boiled, frozen near absolute zero, dried out for years, and even exposed to the vacuum and radiation of open space. They manage it by curling into a shrivelled, near-dead state called cryptobiosis. Scientists have revived specimens after decades in deep freeze.

gk.t: Most global trade is still priced in US dollars
gk.teaser: A legacy of a deal struck in 1944.
gk.more: After World War II, the 1944 Bretton Woods agreement pegged major currencies to the US dollar, which was itself convertible to gold. Even after that gold link ended in 1971, the dollar remained the world's dominant reserve and trade currency, especially for oil — the so-called petrodollar. This gives the US enormous financial leverage, which rivals increasingly try to reduce.

gk.t: The Rule of 72 tells you when your money doubles
gk.teaser: Just divide 72 by the interest rate.
gk.more: Compound interest means you earn returns on your past returns, so money snowballs over time. A handy shortcut, the Rule of 72, estimates how long an investment takes to double: divide 72 by the annual return. At 8% a year, money doubles in about nine years — which is why starting early beats investing large sums late.

gk.t: The first webcam was invented to watch a coffee pot
gk.teaser: Cambridge scientists were tired of empty pots.
gk.more: In 1991, researchers at Cambridge University pointed a camera at a coffee pot so they could check from their desks whether it was full before walking over. Connected to the web in 1993, it became the world's first webcam. The famous 'Trojan Room coffee pot' was switched off in 2001 and auctioned online.

gkMore.teaser: A harmless preview teaches your immune system the enemy's face.
gkMore.more: Vaccines present a dead, weakened, or fragment version of a pathogen (or, in mRNA vaccines, instructions to build one protein of it), so your body develops antibodies without the disease. Smallpox — which killed ~300 million people in the 20th century alone — became the first disease ever eradicated, in 1980.

gkMore.teaser: India is still crashing into Asia at ~5 cm a year.
gkMore.more: Around 50 million years ago the Indian plate slammed into the Eurasian plate; the crumple zone is the Himalayas, still rising a few millimetres yearly. The same collision makes the region earthquake-prone — the reason Delhi sits in seismic zone IV.

gkMore.teaser: Air scatters blue light more than red.
gkMore.more: Sunlight contains all colours; molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter (bluer) wavelengths far more — called Rayleigh scattering. At sunset, light travels through more air, the blue scatters away before reaching you, leaving reds and oranges.

gkMore.teaser: Gravity so strong that light can't leave.
gkMore.more: When a massive star collapses, its gravity can warp space so severely nothing escapes past the 'event horizon'. They're detected by their effect on nearby matter. The first-ever photo of one (M87*, 2019) took a telescope network the size of Earth.

gkMore.teaser: It saves ships ~7,000 km between Europe and Asia.
gkMore.more: Opened in 1869 through Egypt, it carries about 12% of global trade. When one ship (Ever Given) wedged sideways for six days in 2021, it blocked ~$10 billion of goods per day — a live demo of how fragile supply chains are.

Babur founded Mughal rule in 1526; it peaked under Akbar and Aurangzeb. The East India Company's victory at Plassey (1757) began company rule; after the 1857 revolt, the British Crown took direct control ('the Raj') until independence in 1947.

gkMore.teaser: India's Cold War stance: neither US bloc nor Soviet bloc.
gkMore.more: Co-founded by Nehru, Egypt's Nasser and Yugoslavia's Tito in 1961, NAM let newly independent nations avoid picking sides. In practice India tilted toward Soviet defence supplies after the 1962 China war and 1971 US support for Pakistan.

gkMore.teaser: ~10–15 million displaced; up to a million dead.
gkMore.more: British India split into India and Pakistan along the hastily drawn Radcliffe Line, published two days after independence. It remains one of history's largest forced migrations and the root of the Kashmir dispute and three India-Pakistan wars (1947, 1965, 1971 — the last creating Bangladesh).

gkMore.teaser: Earth's year is 365.2422 days — the calendar has to catch up.
gkMore.more: Adding Feb 29 every four years overcorrects slightly, so century years skip it unless divisible by 400 (2000 was a leap year; 2100 won't be). Without leap years, seasons would drift — in ~700 years, June would be winter in Delhi.

gkMore.teaser: You don't vote for President — MPs and MLAs do.
gkMore.more: India's President is elected by an electoral college of elected MPs and state MLAs, with votes weighted by population. Real executive power sits with the PM and council of ministers; the President's key discretionary moments come in hung parliaments and constitutional crises.

learn.title: Natural selection: the simple idea behind life's diversity
learn.teaser: How blind variation builds astonishing design.
body: Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection rests on a surprisingly simple mechanism. Within any population, individuals vary, and some of that variation is inherited by their offspring. When a particular trait helps individuals survive and reproduce in their environment, it becomes more common over generations, while unhelpful traits fade. Given enough time, this gradual filtering can transform species and produce structures as intricate as the eye.

A common misunderstanding is that evolution is purely random. Mutation, which supplies the raw variation, is random — but selection is not. It consistently favours whatever works better in a given setting, giving evolution direction without any need for a designer. All living things, from bacteria to blue whales, share common ancestry, a fact now confirmed in exquisite detail by comparing their DNA.

Evolution is not just history; it happens around us in real time. Bacteria evolving resistance to antibiotics, and viruses mutating into new variants, are natural selection playing out within human lifetimes — and a serious practical challenge for medicine. Understanding the process helps explain not only where the diversity of life came from, but why some of our most powerful drugs steadily lose their punch.

learn.title: Einstein's relativity, in plain language
learn.teaser: Time and space are not the fixed backdrop we assume.
body: In 1905, Albert Einstein published his theory of special relativity, built on a startling premise: the speed of light is the same for every observer, no matter how fast they are moving. To keep that true, something else has to give — and what gives is our commonsense idea that time and space are absolute. A clock on a fast-moving spacecraft genuinely ticks slower than one at rest, an effect called time dilation. The same theory yields the most famous equation in science, E=mc², showing that mass and energy are interchangeable.

A decade later, Einstein extended these ideas into general relativity, a new theory of gravity. Instead of a mysterious force pulling objects together, gravity is reimagined as the bending of spacetime by mass and energy. A massive body like the Sun warps the space around it, and planets simply follow the straightest possible paths through that curved geometry. This picture predicted phenomena that seemed outlandish at the time, including black holes and ripples in spacetime called gravitational waves.

These are not just abstractions. The GPS in your phone must correct for relativistic effects, because clocks on orbiting satellites run at a slightly different rate than clocks on the ground; ignore relativity and navigation would drift off by kilometres within a day. Gravitational waves, predicted in 1916, were directly detected for the first time in 2015, a century later — a stunning confirmation of Einstein's vision.

learn.title: DNA: the four-letter code that builds every living thing
learn.teaser: A molecular instruction manual written in chemistry.
body: Inside almost every cell of every living organism is DNA, a long molecule shaped like a twisted ladder — the famous double helix. The information it carries is written in an alphabet of just four chemical 'letters,' known by the initials A, T, G and C. The precise order of these letters along the strand spells out instructions, much as sequences of letters spell out words and sentences. Stretches of this code called genes act as recipes for building proteins, the molecular machines that run our bodies.

The scale is remarkable in both directions. The human genome contains roughly three billion letters and around 20,000 genes, yet we share the overwhelming majority of our DNA with other humans and a surprisingly large fraction with creatures as different as mice, bananas and yeast — a direct signature of shared ancestry. The Human Genome Project, completed in 2003, first read out the entire human sequence, opening a new era of biology and medicine.

Understanding this code has become one of the most powerful tools in science. Sequencing DNA helps diagnose disease, trace ancestry and track outbreaks, while gene-editing technologies like CRISPR now allow scientists to rewrite specific letters of the code with precision. That power raises profound possibilities — curing inherited diseases — alongside serious ethical questions about how far we should go in editing the blueprint of life.

learn.title: The greenhouse effect and a warming planet
learn.teaser: The physics is old; the scale is new.
body: Earth stays warm enough for life because certain gases in the atmosphere — carbon dioxide, methane, water vapour — let sunlight in but trap some of the heat the planet radiates back out, like a blanket. This natural greenhouse effect is essential; without it, Earth would be a frozen ball. The problem is not the effect itself but its intensification: by burning coal, oil and gas, humanity has raised atmospheric carbon dioxide to levels not seen in millions of years.

The result is a measurable warming of the planet — roughly 1.2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels so far — which sounds small but reshapes the climate system. It means more frequent and intense heatwaves, droughts and heavy downpours, rising sea levels as ice melts and oceans expand, and disruption to weather patterns. For India, one of the gravest risks is to the monsoon, on which agriculture and water supplies for over a billion people depend.

In response, nearly all nations signed the 2015 Paris Agreement, pledging to limit warming well below 2 degrees. Countries have set 'net-zero' targets — the point at which they add no more greenhouse gases than they remove — with India aiming for 2070 while rapidly expanding solar and wind power. The central challenge of the coming decades is to keep growing economies and lifting people out of poverty while steeply cutting emissions.

learn.title: How the Green Revolution changed India's food story
learn.teaser: From famine fears to grain surpluses.
body: In the 1960s, India faced a food crisis. Its population was growing fast, harvests were poor, and the country depended on imported grain and food aid to stave off famine. The turning point came with the Green Revolution: the introduction of new high-yielding varieties of wheat and rice, developed by scientists including Norman Borlaug and, in India, M. S. Swaminathan, combined with chemical fertilisers, pesticides, and expanded irrigation.

The impact was transformative. States like Punjab, Haryana and western Uttar Pradesh became the nation's breadbaskets, grain output soared, and within a couple of decades India moved from the brink of famine to self-sufficiency, and eventually to being a food exporter. It is widely credited with saving millions from hunger and giving the young nation a foundation of food security.

But the revolution carried real costs that are now pressing concerns. Intensive irrigation has drained groundwater to alarming depths in the northwest; heavy fertiliser and pesticide use has degraded soils and polluted water; and the focus on a few staple crops narrowed diets and cropping diversity. The benefits were also unevenly spread across regions. India's current challenge is a 'second' revolution — one that sustains high yields while repairing the environmental damage of the first.

learn.title: What happens when you load a web page
learn.teaser: The elegant plumbing beneath every click.
body: When you type an address and hit enter, your request does not travel as one continuous stream. Instead, the data is broken into small chunks called packets, each individually addressed and sent off across the network. Guided by a family of rules known as TCP/IP, these packets hop from router to router — often taking different paths — and are reassembled in the correct order at the destination. There is no central controller directing traffic; the system is deliberately decentralised.

Two pieces of infrastructure make this feel instant. The Domain Name System (DNS) acts like the internet's phone book, translating a human-friendly name like a website's address into the numerical IP address computers actually use to find each other. And most intercontinental traffic does not go by satellite but through a web of fibre-optic cables lying on the ocean floor, carrying data between continents at nearly the speed of light.

This architecture is a direct inheritance from the internet's origins in ARPANET, which was designed so that data could reroute around damaged nodes and keep flowing. That resilience is why the network scales so gracefully and rarely goes fully dark. Every web page you open is the product of thousands of these tiny packets racing across the planet and back, usually in a fraction of a second.

learn.title: The AI shift: what large language models actually do
learn.teaser: Powerful pattern machines, not digital minds.
body: The recent wave of artificial intelligence is driven by large language models (LLMs) — the technology behind modern AI chatbots. At their core, these systems are trained on vast quantities of text and learn to do something deceptively simple: predict the next word in a sequence. By doing this billions of times over enormous datasets, they absorb the statistical patterns of language, along with a great deal of the knowledge encoded in that text, and can then generate fluent, often strikingly useful responses.

It helps to be clear about what this is and isn't. An LLM does not 'understand' the world the way a person does, nor does it look up facts in a database. It generates plausible continuations based on patterns, which is why it can be articulate and helpful but also confidently wrong — producing 'hallucinations,' or inheriting biases present in its training data. The breakthrough that made today's models possible was a neural-network design called the transformer, introduced in 2017, combined with vast computing power.

The economic and social implications are still unfolding. AI is already reshaping how people write, code, research and create, and it poses particular questions for economies like India's, with its large services and IT sector. The realistic near-term picture is less science-fiction robot and more a powerful general-purpose tool — one that amplifies human work, demands new skills, and requires care about accuracy, fairness and appropriate use.

learn.title: Why tiny chips decide global power
learn.teaser: The most important industry most people never think about.
body: Semiconductors — the microchips inside phones, cars, appliances, weapons and data centres — have become the foundation of the modern economy. What makes them geopolitically explosive is how extraordinarily concentrated their production is. The most advanced chips are manufactured by a handful of firms, above all Taiwan's TSMC, and the ultra-precise machines needed to etch them are made by essentially one company, ASML in the Netherlands, while much of the cutting-edge design comes from the United States.

This means no single country can make a leading-edge chip alone; the supply chain is a globally intertwined web with critical choke points. That fragility became painfully visible during the shortages of 2021, when a shortfall of chips idled car factories worldwide. It has also turned semiconductors into a central front in the rivalry between the United States and China, with export controls on advanced chips and chipmaking equipment used as tools of statecraft.

Taiwan's dominance in particular gives the island outsized strategic importance and adds a dangerous edge to tensions over its status. Governments have responded by pouring money into building domestic chip factories, or 'fabs,' to reduce their dependence — including India, which is courting fabrication and assembly plants as part of its push to become a manufacturing hub. In the coming decades, who makes the world's chips may matter as much as who controls its oil once did.
